Closes #290

## Motivation

What this PR does is change the way PyT finds vulnerabilities, as mentioned in the [docs](http://pyt.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html) and the [thesis](http://projekter.aau.dk/projekter/files/239563289/final.pdf#page=57), PyT originally used reaching definitions -- [slightly modified](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/pyt/reaching_definitions_taint.py#L15) to keep reassignments going (i.e. keeping the definition reaching) and, ran this slightly modified reaching definitions on [every 'secondary' node](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/ab24ad7f8dda7c55b377b0370dd3343e9fe0fa03/pyt/vulnerabilities.py#L231) in addition to the [source](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/ab24ad7f8dda7c55b377b0370dd3343e9fe0fa03/pyt/vulnerabilities.py#L226).

The thing I wanted to do, was handle '[blackbox](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/pull/63)' function calls well, does `url_for` with a tainted argument yield a tainted value? What about `os.path.join`? Or `some_random_web_framework_function`? Having an answer would have helped us in [our last evaluation](http://pyt.readthedocs.io/en/latest/past_evaluations.html).

There is a problem with only knowing that a definition reaches a sink, and not how. You can know `arg = request.args.get('foo')` reaches `subprocess.check_output`, but if there are multiple paths from source to sink, there may be one path that's vulnerable and one that's not. i.e. If there is a blackbox node that doesn't propagate taint or a sanitizer along the path we don't want to say it "might" not be vulnerable just because we don't know the path. You can see an example in the [`multi_chain.py` test](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/example/vulnerable_code/multi_chain.py#L16) with [corresponding mapping](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/pyt/vulnerability_definitions/blackbox_mapping.json#L7). I use 'path' and 'chain' interchangeably.

It's also [confusing](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/issues/46) IMHO to just see all of the secondary nodes in the output as opposed to the ones that lead to the vulnerability, I tried to remedy this by implementing the trim option, which [stepped backwards along one path](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/pull/47/files#diff-78e00ea54b71da081bc34a739c96278fR259), but that might not be the vulnerable path. Though I still [left the default as secondary the nodes](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/pyt/vulnerabilities.py#L428) if you don't have the trim or interactive flag on.

This also gives us the opportunity to [ask the user](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/pyt/vulnerabilities.py#L347) about if a blackbox function we haven't encountered propagates taint, (if they set the interactive command line option) and then save the mapping to a config, since we can't know all the web frameworks functions in the world.

## How It Works

The way this works is by, after knowing that either a source or a secondary node reaches a sink (i.e. doing what we've always done), we construct a [definition-use mapping](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/pyt/definition_chains.py#L54-L72), and traversing through the [uses of the source, then the defs of those uses, and so on and so forth until the use is the sink](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/pyt/vulnerabilities.py#L282-L307). This was a great opportunity to use `yield from` and recursion, once we have a 'chain' we pass it to how_vulnerable which checks the chain against the blackbox mapping and sanitizers, if it comes back as FALSE we loop through the other chains and [call `how_vulnerable` on until we find a vulnerable one](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/pyt/vulnerabilities.py#L442-L457).

We now use a [vuln_factory](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/pyt/vulnerability_helper.py#L173-L179) and pass a dictionary, [vuln_deets](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/pyt/vulnerabilities.py#L423-L429), as args.


## Additional details 

We can tell it's [definitely](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/pyt/vulnerabilities.py#L337) sanitized when the sanitizer is [an assignment node](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/pyt/vulnerabilities.py#L436). Right now we can only say [potentially](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/tests/vulnerabilities_test.py#L326) if the sanitizer is an `if` statement, this is because we're currently not smart enough to tell if e.g. [the condition leads to a return](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/example/vulnerable_code/path_traversal_sanitised_2.py#L11). I am okay with this.

## Future Work

* Being able to tell if the tainted argument is at the beginning or end of the string that ends up in the sink.
```python
def client_passport():
    code = request.args.get('code')
    uri = 'http://localhost:5000/oauth?response_type=%s&client_id=%s&redirect_uri=%s' %(code,client_id,redirect_uri)
    return redirect(uri)
```
was a false-positive in our [last evaluation](http://pyt.readthedocs.io/en/latest/past_evaluations.html) due to this, this is also helpful for [SSRF](http://www.levigross.com/2014/07/04/security-concerns-with-pythons-urllib-and-urllib2/) and other vulnerabilities.

* Currently in this PR we stop and return a vulnerability [if it's not FALSE](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/blob/3df4c288d82b308bf4d9cd62a8ff3c3e6ba34e26/pyt/vulnerabilities.py#L456), I'll change this in the future to be better and keep on going if the first thing we find is sanitized or unknown (and the e.g. 2nd thing we find is a TRUE vulnerability.)

* JSON output

* Maybe using textwrap for the vulnerability descriptions, only if it looks better.

* I also might implement a command line option to list all of the vulnerable paths, though this is low-priority as it won't help us in our future evaluations.

pr_title: [V3] handle time innacuracy by warning owner
pr_body:
### Type

- [ ] Bugfix
- [X] Enhancement
- [ ] New feature

### Description of the changes

We shouldn't need to build in code checks everywhere for inaccurate system time. It is the responsibility of the host to ensure this. This adds a warning on a detected system clock inaccuracy that any time-sensitive code in the bot may fail if detecting a significant difference in discord message times to the system clock.

### Type

- [X] Bugfix
- [ ] Enhancement
- [ ] New feature

### Description of the changes

This fixes help for command subgroups which use an `isinstance` to determine they are the last thing in the chain. A more robust fix for this would require more extensive modifications to `redbot.core.commands`

I rewrite build windows wheels using https://conda.io/miniconda.html, it can build python2.7 and 3.5, 32bit and 64bit successfully in one command.

I fix the `ImportError: DLL load failed`  by `data_files` in `setup.py`, it will copy the dll file to site-packages on installing.

Usage:
1. install 32bit and 64 bit miniconda (whatever 2.7 and 3.5 is OK), it can be download from https://conda.io/miniconda.html
2. install invoke using `pip install invoke`, whatever version python is OK
3. modify `tools/windows/tasks.py`, change `CONDA_32` and `CONDA_64` to corresponding conda.exe in your system
4. `cd tools/windows` and `inv --echo build_all`

To check out this pull request, see https://help.github.com/articles/checking-out-pull-requests-locally/

pr_title: [raw/enrich] Handle long description in Jira fields
pr_body: This PR allows to handle long fields.description values in the raw and enriched indexes. In the raw index, a mapping is defined to to avoid immense term exceptions, while in the enriched index, the value is truncated to avoid changing the type of the attribute (keyword).
